texts.orig: CO2 capture from gas mixtures is important in modern society. A fluorinated MOFbased membrane was made only permeable to CO2, blocking the permeation of both smaller (H2) and larger (N2, CH4) molecules. Such CO2-recognition behavior presents great promise for carbon capture from varied gas streams.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: SUMMARY
texts.text: SUMMARY

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The steady removal of carbon dioxide (CO2) from diverse gas streams is a critical step toward achieving the blueprint of carbon neutrality and clean energy production. However, the associated energy and capital inputs are considerably high, necessitating the development of effective technologies for CO2 separation. Here, we report a special CO2-recognition membrane based on a fluorinated metal-organic framework (KAUST-7) for efficient CO2 capture from various mixtures including CO2/H2, CO2/CH4, and CO2/N2. Uniquely, the appropriate CO2 affinity cooperating with the confined aperture of KAUST-7 enables the membrane with the right orientation to be nearly only permeable to CO2, showing unprecedented CO2 separation selectivity over both smaller (H2) and larger (N2, CH4) molecules. The extraordinary performance is maintained after treatment with corrosive hydrogen sulfide or humid atmospheres, swing temperatures or pressures, and long-term operations, pinpointing the potential for high-throughput CO2 capture in a continuous mode.
texts.text: The steady removal of carbon dioxide (CO2) from diverse gas streams is a critical step toward achieving the blueprint of carbon neutrality and clean energy production. However, the associated energy and capital inputs are considerably high, necessitating the development of effective technologies for CO2 separation. Here, we report a special CO2-recognition membrane based on a fluorinated metal-organic framework (KAUST-7) for efficient CO2 capture from various mixtures including CO2/H2, CO2/CH4, and CO2/N2. Uniquely, the appropriate CO2 affinity cooperating with the confined aperture of KAUST-7 enables the membrane with the right orientation to be nearly only permeable to CO2, showing unprecedented CO2 separation selectivity over both smaller (H2) and larger (N2, CH4) molecules. The extraordinary performance is maintained after treatment with corrosive hydrogen sulfide or humid atmospheres, swing temperatures or pressures, and long-term operations, pinpointing the potential for high-throughput CO2 capture in a continuous mode.

texts.text: INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The efficient separation of carbon dioxide (CO2) from various gas streams is crucial for both the carbon neutrality and the clean-energy future. 1 On one hand, CO2 emitted from fossil fuel combustion accounts for approximately 60% of global anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions, which is responsible, at least in part, for the global climate change. 2 An ambitious blueprint of carbon neutrality has been set to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050. 3 One of the promising strategies is to selectively capture CO2 from the post-combustion flue gas streams before they are released into the atmosphere. 4 On the other hand, as the idea of clean energy gains popularity, the focus on hydrogen (H2) economy, or methane (CH4) as a transitional fuel, is increasing. 5,6 In industrial processes to produce CH4 and H2, such as natural-gas purification, biogas upgrading, steam methane reforming, and water-gas shifted reaction, CO2 exists as the major impurity in the gas mixtures and needs to be removed effectively. 7,8 However, despite the prime importance, the development of viable capture technologies capable of lowering the energy consumptions and capital costs associated with the CO2 separation process is particularly challenging and technically difficult. 8

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: THE BIGGER PICTURE
texts.text: THE BIGGER PICTURE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide (CO2) capture is critical to reaching the global goal of net-zero emissions by 2050. A cost-effective and economically productive pathway is using membrane technology to selectively remove CO2 from the gas mixture. Here, we report a fluorinated metal-organic framework (MOF)-based membranecapable of recognizing and capturing CO2 only from the gas streams, regardless of the molecular sizes of other gases. Our study discloses that such unique behavior originates from the cooperative contributions of the adsorption affinity and molecular sieving effect. The resulting pure MOF-based membrane exhibits ultra-high CO2 selectivity over other related gases, such as hydrogen (H2), nitrogen (N2), and methane (CH4). The concept of cooperative separation could be a model to design membranes with ultra-high permselectivity.

The resulting pure MOF-based membrane exhibits ultra-high CO2 selectivity over other related gases, such as hydrogen (H2), nitrogen (N2), and methane (CH4). The concept of cooperative separation could be a model to design membranes with ultra-high permselectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane-based separation technology offers the desired potential and a constant flux of CO2 would be formed during the membrane permeation, which is driven by the pressure difference. 10 To date, the membrane market pertaining to CO2 separations is dominated by the polymer membranes. Typically, the applied organic polymers are CO2 affinitive and the gas permeation follows the solution-diffusion mechanism. 11 However, challenges associated with the plasticization and tradeoffs between the permeability and selectivity for the polymer materials have limited their working range and capacity in the real-world operation conditions. 12 As an alternative, membranes constructed from crystalline nanoporous materials with permanent porosity, tailor-made pore chemistry, and fine-tuned pore-aperture sizes, such as MOFs, are promising to break the upper bounds and provide better separation performance. 13,14 In general, the primary consideration for the design of such kind of porous membranes is to match the pore-aperture size of the material with the kinetic diameter of the targeted gas mixtures. 15 In this case, the pore-apertures serve as the sole barrier and the separation is based on the transportation kinetics difference. Nevertheless, among the extensive studies on MOF membranes, few of them could show impressive CO2 separation as expected, and most of them provided moderate selectivity only, despite that their pore-apertures are theoretically suitable for CO2 separation. One of the major reasons is that the framework flexibility 16 or structure defects 17 could cause expanded pore-aperture size and provide comparable opportunities for the second species in the mixtures to pass through, leading to competitive diffusion and decreased selectivity.

Typically, the applied organic polymers are CO2 affinitive and the gas permeation follows the solution-diffusion mechanism. 11 However, challenges associated with the plasticization and tradeoffs between the permeability and selectivity for the polymer materials have limited their working range and capacity in the real-world operation conditions. 12 As an alternative, membranes constructed from crystalline nanoporous materials with permanent porosity, tailor-made pore chemistry, and fine-tuned pore-aperture sizes, such as MOFs, are promising to break the upper bounds and provide better separation performance. 13,14 In general, the primary consideration for the design of such kind of porous membranes is to match the pore-aperture size of the material with the kinetic diameter of the targeted gas mixtures. 15 In this case, the pore-apertures serve as the sole barrier and the separation is based on the transportation kinetics difference. Nevertheless, among the extensive studies on MOF membranes, few of them could show impressive CO2 separation as expected, and most of them provided moderate selectivity only, despite that their pore-apertures are theoretically suitable for CO2 separation. One of the major reasons is that the framework flexibility 16 or structure defects 17 could cause expanded pore-aperture size and provide comparable opportunities for the second species in the mixtures to pass through, leading to competitive diffusion and decreased selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accordingly, relying on the pore-aperture size only is not adequate to reach the desired separation performance, and a secondary strategy is needed to minimize the chance for the other gases to enter the pore systems. Taking inspiration from polymer membranes, we propose that introducing the specific affinity pertaining to CO2 into the pore surface, namely the strong CO2 interactions with the host frameworks during the CO2 physisorption, 18 which is viable for the MOF platforms by crystal engineering approach, which could empower CO2 with the top priority to penetrate, regardless of the sizes of other competing gases. Collaboratively, by narrowing the channels until only one gas molecule could be accommodated, we are theoretically able to construct a specialized highway for CO2, where CO2 itself acts as a secondary barrier to block the diffusion of other gases including both smaller and larger molecules.

The resultant membrane is nearly only permeable to CO2 and shows record-high CO2 separation selectivity over H2, CH4, and N2, with the best mixture separation factors reaching 70, 180, and 350, respectively. texts.label: text
texts.orig: KAUST-7 is constructed from a two-dimensional Ni-pyrazine square grid layer and further pillared by the NbOF5 2 � in the third dimension to form a three-dimensional network (Figure 1A). The sole entrance for gas molecules is the straight channel with a crystallographic aperture size of 3.2 A ˚ along the c direction, whereas the other two sides/surfaces are nonporous. 18,19 Since the flux of membranes is closely correlated with the effective mass transfer distance, the shortest pathway can be constructed by aligning the straight one-dimensional channels parallel to the molecule permeation direction, 20 which requires our KAUST-7 membranes to be c-oriented. We tried to prepare oriented KAUST-7 nanosheets as a seed layer but it was unsuccessful, mainly due to the strong nucleophilic ability of the NbOF5 2 � and thus the fast growth along the c direction. By contrast, the isoreticular structure based on the AlF5 2 � pillar, namely AlFFIVE-1-Ni (KAUST-8, Figure 1A), is easier to form c-oriented nanosheets via simply accelerating the reaction rate. 21 Since both the KAUST-7 and KAUST-8 share the same Ni-pyrazine square grid as the supramolecular building layer, the matched lattice between them enables the deployment of KAUST-8 nanosheets as a template to construct oriented KAUST-7 crystals and membranes (Figure 1B).
texts.text: KAUST-7 is constructed from a two-dimensional Ni-pyrazine square grid layer and further pillared by the NbOF5 2 � in the third dimension to form a three-dimensional network (Figure 1A). The sole entrance for gas molecules is the straight channel with a crystallographic aperture size of 3.2 A ˚ along the c direction, whereas the other two sides/surfaces are nonporous. 18,19 Since the flux of membranes is closely correlated with the effective mass transfer distance, the shortest pathway can be constructed by aligning the straight one-dimensional channels parallel to the molecule permeation direction, 20 which requires our KAUST-7 membranes to be c-oriented. We tried to prepare oriented KAUST-7 nanosheets as a seed layer but it was unsuccessful, mainly due to the strong nucleophilic ability of the NbOF5 2 � and thus the fast growth along the c direction. By contrast, the isoreticular structure based on the AlF5 2 � pillar, namely AlFFIVE-1-Ni (KAUST-8, Figure 1A), is easier to form c-oriented nanosheets via simply accelerating the reaction rate. 21 Since both the KAUST-7 and KAUST-8 share the same Ni-pyrazine square grid as the supramolecular building layer, the matched lattice between them enables the deployment of KAUST-8 nanosheets as a template to construct oriented KAUST-7 crystals and membranes (Figure 1B).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, square-shaped KAUST-8 nanosheets could be obtained by stirring overnight the mixture solution containing Ni(NO3)2 $ 6H2O, pyrazine, Al(OH)3, hydrofluoric acid, and H2O at room temperature. The obtained nanosheets are transparent under a scanning electronic microscope (SEM), with a lateral size of 1-2 m mandathickness of  13 nm (Figure 1C). A clear Tyndall effect for the nanosheet dispersion in ethanol could be observed, consistent with other high-quality nanosheets 22 (Figure 1C). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the KAUST-8 nanosheets shows only two peaks related to (002) and (004) planes, respectively, matching well with the simulated c-oriented KAUST-8 structure (Figure S1). KAUST-8 nanosheets are then transferred onto the porous alumina (Al2O3) support to form a seed layer by a simple dip-coating method. As observed from the top-view SEM image, the support surface has been fully covered by KAUST-8 nanosheets (Figure 1D), and the cross-section SEM image reveals the seed layer is stacked by 1-3 sheets (Figure 1D). The XRD pattern of the seeded supports shows that the orientation of KAUST-8 nanosheets is well preserved (Figure S2), indicating the pore channel is perpendicular to the support surface.

texts.orig: In order to stabilize the grain boundary structure, we develop a ''steam-assisted surface reorganization'' strategy to in situ break the sensitive crystals and rebuild the crystal interface. Considering the hemilability of KAUST-7 under high-temperature humid conditions, 23 a relatively mild steam condition imposed on the pristine membranes will promote the disintegration of the most sensitive crystals. According to the Ostwald ripening theory, the segmented building blocks tend to assemble into larger crystals, thus fusing the crystal interface and stabilizing the grain boundary structure. 24 Such a mild steam treatment condition can be created by placing liquid water and the as-synthesized membrane together inside a sealed bottle and then heating at 85 o C. As revealed by the top-view SEM image, the membrane treated for 8 h presents a quite smooth surface morphology (Figure 2B). The original small and protruding crystals disappear and the crystal boundary becomes ambiguous, indicating the grain boundary structure has been successfully reorganized. An elegant thin-film interference phenomenon that could be observed by the naked eye also supports the formation of a smooth surface after surface reorganization 15 (Figure S6). However, further extending the steam treatment period does not help to obtain a more robust structure because the crystals themselves would be damaged, as revealed by the corresponding SEM image (Figure 2C). The XRD pattern of the steam-treated membrane shows that the crystallinity of KAUST-7 is well preserved, whereas that of KAUST-8 is weakened a lot (Figure S7). In addition, a new peak related to NiAlF5 pillar salt appears, indicating the underlying KAUST-8 nanosheets are less stable and suffer from partial decomposition during the steam treatment. This could be a positive transformation for membrane permeation since the transportation resistance through KAUST-8 becomes negligible.
texts.text: In order to stabilize the grain boundary structure, we develop a ''steam-assisted surface reorganization'' strategy to in situ break the sensitive crystals and rebuild the crystal interface. Considering the hemilability of KAUST-7 under high-temperature humid conditions, 23 a relatively mild steam condition imposed on the pristine membranes will promote the disintegration of the most sensitive crystals. According to the Ostwald ripening theory, the segmented building blocks tend to assemble into larger crystals, thus fusing the crystal interface and stabilizing the grain boundary structure. 24 Such a mild steam treatment condition can be created by placing liquid water and the as-synthesized membrane together inside a sealed bottle and then heating at 85 o C. As revealed by the top-view SEM image, the membrane treated for 8 h presents a quite smooth surface morphology (Figure 2B). The original small and protruding crystals disappear and the crystal boundary becomes ambiguous, indicating the grain boundary structure has been successfully reorganized. An elegant thin-film interference phenomenon that could be observed by the naked eye also supports the formation of a smooth surface after surface reorganization 15 (Figure S6). However, further extending the steam treatment period does not help to obtain a more robust structure because the crystals themselves would be damaged, as revealed by the corresponding SEM image (Figure 2C). The XRD pattern of the steam-treated membrane shows that the crystallinity of KAUST-7 is well preserved, whereas that of KAUST-8 is weakened a lot (Figure S7). In addition, a new peak related to NiAlF5 pillar salt appears, indicating the underlying KAUST-8 nanosheets are less stable and suffer from partial decomposition during the steam treatment. This could be a positive transformation for membrane permeation since the transportation resistance through KAUST-8 becomes negligible.

texts.orig: Compared with the ideal selectivity, all the mixed-gas separation factors are improved a lot. Manifestly, the strong CO2 affinity of KAUST-7 promotes CO2 to penetrate into the pore channels with the top priority, 18 and then the CO2 occupies the pathway as a dynamic barrier since the extremely narrowed channels can only accommodate one molecule, leading to the exclusion of other less affinitive gases. 25 Consequently, the special CO2 recognition behavior is presented due to the optimal balance between the adsorption affinity and molecular sieving effect. This proposed separation process is evidently supported by the CO2/H2 separation behavior. For the single-gas permeation without competition, CO2 and H2 present similar permeance, showing no selectivity between them (Figure 3A). While for the mixed-gas permeation with strong competition, even though the size of H2 is smaller than that of CO2, the permeation of H2 is severely blocked by CO2, leading to an enhanced and reversed CO2/H2 separation selectivity (Figure 3B). This result is consistent with our previously reported SIFSIX-3-Ni and SIFSIX-3-Cu membranes with reversed CO2/H2 separation selectivity as well. 25 Notably, using a CO2-selective membrane for the practical CO2/H2 separation is of great importance in industry since a highly CO2-selective membrane that allows CO2 to permeate enables almost 100% CO conversion in an equilibrium-dominated low-temperature water-gas shift reactor, which reduces the H2 losses during separation 27 ; meanwhile, when producing high-purity H2 by CO2/H2 separation at industry-relevant high feed pressures, a highly CO2-selective membrane could retain H2 in the high-pressure side, which is energetically favorable and could save the high cost of recompression compared with the cost incurred by using H2-selective membranes. 27,28 It is to be noted that a recent work reporting KAUST-7 membranes (without steam-assisted surface reorganization) showed the normal H2/CO2 selectivity and high H2 permeance, which might be caused by the non-selective defects within the membranes (Note S1, Figures S13 and S14), as evidenced by the respective low selectivity over N2 or CH4. 29

texts.orig: Moreover, the huge improvement of mixed-gas selectivity for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 mixtures relative to the single-gas permeation, in this work, is also resulted from the same cooperative mechanism (Figures 3C and 3D). However, although both N2 and CH4 are substantially blocked by CO2 and the size of CH4 is slightly larger than that of N2, the CO2/CH4 separation factor is actually lower than CO2/N2, due to the relatively better affinity with the framework for CH4 and thus weakened selectivity contribution from adsorption. Indeed, there are some previous cases translating CO2 adsorbents into porous membranes, such as MOF-74 membranes, 30 amine-appended MOF-74 membranes, 30 and ZIF-100 membranes. 31 However, all those previous cases failed to display such a permeation priority for CO2, which on the other hand proved the uniqueness of KAUST-7 membranes and the associated cooperative separation mechanism by optimally balancing the adsorption affinity with molecular sieving effect.

For all three gas pairs, the separation factors decrease gradually with the temperature increasing from 25 o Cto 125 o C(Figure 4). After cooling down to 25 o C, the performance can fully recover, indicating that the changes resulted from the intrinsic properties instead of membrane damages. texts.orig: Wefurther evaluated the membrane performance under different practical conditions. Usually, the CO2 concentration in the feed stream is dependent on different sources. 8 For example, biogas contains roughly 50% CO2, whereas the flue gas contains 12%15% CO2 in the case of coal emissions or 4% CO2 from natural gas combined-cycle emissions. 10 Therefore, the membrane's CO2 separation ability in different practical concentrations is one of the key criteria to consider. Taking the CO2/N2 mixed-gas separation as a case study, we evaluated the performances with the CO2 concentration varying from 1% to 90%. Distinctly, lower CO2 concentration leads to higher CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 separation factor, which is totally different with other MOF materials such as MOF-5 (Figure 5A). For the 1% CO2/99% N2 feed, the KAUST-7 membranes show a CO2 permeance of 168 GPU and an extremely high CO2/N2 separation factor of 510. This special behavior is determined by the strong CO2 affinity of KAUST7, which makes it the best material for trace CO2 capture. 18 As a result, even when the CO2 concentration is very low, the pore channels of the membranes can still be easily occupied. However, higher CO2 concentration leads to lower CO2 permeance and thus lower CO2/N2 separation factor, due to non-linear adsorption behavior and the resulting decreased pressure-normalized flux. Even though, from 1% to 90% CO2 concentration, KAUST-7 membranes can always maintain the unique CO2 recognition property with the mixture selectivity above 300, demonstrating the widest working range for CO2 separation (Figure 5A).

Furthermore, moisture is another possible trace impurity that may coexist with the targeted gas pairs, such as the water-gas shift reaction produced H2, CO2, and H2O mixture. 7,14 We evaluated the CO2/H2 separation with the feed stream humidified by 3% water vapor, to mimic the H2 purification after water-gas shift reaction. 33 Under the humid feed stream, the CO2/H2 separation factor increases from 60 to 90, whereas CO2 permeance decreases slightly (Figure 5C). The decrease in permeance is owing to the strong affinity of KAUST-7 to H2O, which competes with CO2 and H2 and partially blocks their diffusion. 18 The increase in separation factor indicates that this competition influences more for the inert H2. However, shifting the feed stream back to dry feed cannot make the membrane separation performance recover immediately due to the strong interaction of H2O with the framework unless it is regenerated at higher temperatures such as 150 o C (Figure 5C). Notably, after regeneration, the separation performance is consistent with the initial property (Figure 5C), pinpointing the excellent operability of KAUST-7 membranes in humid conditions. Actually, in addition to the CO2/H2 separation, the proved stability under wet gas conditions is critical for CO2/N2 separation as well since flue gas almost always has some water vapor. However, shifting the feed stream back to dry feed cannot make the membrane separation performance recover immediately due to the strong interaction of H2O with the framework unless it is regenerated at higher temperatures such as 150 o C (Figure 5C). Notably, after regeneration, the separation performance is consistent with the initial property (Figure 5C), pinpointing the excellent operability of KAUST-7 membranes in humid conditions. Actually, in addition to the CO2/H2 separation, the proved stability under wet gas conditions is critical for CO2/N2 separation as well since flue gas almost always has some water vapor. It is expected that the

Besides, this membrane is also proved extremely robust during the long-term continuous operation of 35 days, without any aging or selectivity loss (Figure 5D).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, we constructed a special CO2-recognition membrane with c-orientation for efficient CO2 capture, which is nearly only permeable for CO2 and excludes both smaller and larger gases. Record-high mixed-gas selectivities for CO2/H2, CO2/N2, and CO2/CH4 are obtained. This unique behavior results from the optimal combination between adsorption affinity and molecular sieving, where the strong CO2 affinity guarantees CO2 to penetrate into and occupy the narrowed one-dimensional channels always with the top priority, and further acts as a dynamic barrier to exclude any other molecules. This cooperative mechanism is understood by investigating different gas pairs as well as their temperature-swing behaviors. The KAUST-7 membranes present a wide working range for different CO2 concentrations and varied feed pressures. Also, under practically extreme conditions containing corrosive H2S or water vapor, the membranes can still maintain excellent performance. This efficient CO2-recognition behavior can present a new concept for membrane design and bring great promise for practical CO2 capture.
